name: Citsukha
dates_notes: Dated by guru-paramparā and citation by datable later authors; commonly placed c. 1220 CE.
core_thesis: Citsukha brings Vivaraṇa-Advaita to its dialectical maturity. Ānandabodha's inferential apparatus is refashioned into a comprehensive *khaṇḍana* of every realist alternative on offer: pre-Gaṅgeśa Nyāya, Mīmāṃsā, Bhedābheda, and the proto-Dvaita and Viśiṣṭādvaita positions then crystallising. Four technical doctrines anchor the work. First, *cit* / *ātman* is *svaprakāśa*, self-luminous: defined as that which is *aparokṣa-vyavahāra-yogya* (fit for direct cognitive employment) without depending on a further cognition. Second, the world is *anirvacanīya-mithyā*, established by formal *anumāna*; *mithyā* is throughout *sad-asad-vilakṣaṇa* (different from both real and unreal) and never collapses into *asat*. Third, avidyā is *bhāva-rūpā* with Brahman as both locus and object. Fourth, the Nyāya category-scheme is destroyed: *sāmānya*, *viśeṣa*, *samavāya*, and *bheda* itself are shown to be analytically indeterminable. The destruction proceeds by dilemma — for each category, a binary characterisation is exhausted and shown self-defeating, leaving the category itself unintelligible. Citsukha works throughout in the technical idiom of the pre-Gaṅgeśa Naiyāyikas, beating them on their own turf. The *Tattva-Pradīpikā* becomes the standard Advaita reference for the dialectic of *mithyātva* and is presupposed by the entire *Advaita-Siddhi* / *Nyāyāmṛta* exchange.

engaged_works.title: Tattva-Pradipika
engaged_works.summary: Vast polemical *prakaraṇa* — universally cited as the *Citsukhī* — organised in four *paricchedas* and devoted to systematic defence of the Vivaraṇa-Advaita theses against every available realist opponent. The first *paricheda* defines and defends *svaprakāśatva*: *cit* is self-luminous, characterised as *avedyatve sati aparokṣa-vyavahāra-yogyatva* (fit for direct cognitive employment without itself being an object). The second gives the canonical formal *anumānas* for *anirvacanīya-mithyātva*, most famously the *dṛśyatvānumāna*, while insisting throughout that *mithyā* is *sad-asad-vilakṣaṇa* and never *asat*. The third defends *bhāva-rūpā-avidyā* with Brahman as both locus and object. The fourth deconstructs the Nyāya categories. Each category — *sāmānya*, *viśeṣa*, *samavāya*, *bheda* — is met by a destructive dilemma: a closed disjunction of possible analyses is exhausted, each horn shown to be self-defeating or vacuous. *Bheda* in particular is examined as itself a candidate substance, attribute, or relation; each option fails. Bhedābheda and the early Madhva-line account of substantial difference are refuted in the same idiom. Pratyagrūpa's *Nayanaprasādinī* is the standard sub-commentary. The *Citsukhī* becomes the technical encyclopaedia presupposed by Madhusūdana's *Advaita-Siddhi*, by Vyāsatīrtha's *Nyāyāmṛta*, and by every later participant in the *mithyātva* debate.
